# OptiCore

> High-performance options pricing, IV solver, and Greeks. C++20 numerical
> core with a Pythonic API. Black-Scholes-Merton with full machine
> precision, vectorized over NumPy arrays, with batteries-included data
> adapters and plotting helpers. Apache-2.0.

## Install

```bash
pip install opticore                  # core (numpy, pandas)
pip install opticore[viz]             # + matplotlib for plot helpers
pip install opticore[ibkr]            # + Interactive Brokers via ib_async
pip install opticore[data-yfinance]   # + Yahoo Finance (~15-min delayed)
pip install opticore[all]             # everything above
```

Requires Python 3.10–3.13 on Linux, macOS, or Windows. The wheel ships a
prebuilt C++ extension; no compiler needed at install time.

## Public API (9 functions, locked scope)

```python
import opticore as oc

# 1. Price a European call/put
oc.price(spot, strike, expiry, rate, vol, kind="call", div_yield=0.0) -> float | ndarray

# 2. Implied volatility (Newton + bisection, machine precision)
oc.iv(price, spot, strike, expiry, rate, kind="call", div_yield=0.0) -> float | ndarray

# 3. All Greeks in a single pass
oc.greeks(spot, strike, expiry, rate, vol, kind, div_yield) -> GreeksResult
oc.greeks_table(...) -> pd.DataFrame  # vectorized over arrays

# 4. Option chains
oc.fetch_chain(symbol, provider="ibkr" | "yfinance" | "sample", ...) -> pd.DataFrame
oc.enrich(chain, rate, div_yield, include_theo=True) -> pd.DataFrame  # adds IV, Greeks, theo_price, mispricing

# 5. Diagnostics
oc.parity_check(chain, rate, div_yield) -> pd.DataFrame      # put-call parity residuals
oc.implied_forward(chain, rate) -> pd.DataFrame              # F(T) and implied div yield per expiry

# 6. Plotting (returns matplotlib (Figure, Axes))
oc.plot.smile(enriched_df, expiry=None, x="strike")
oc.plot.payoff(legs)            # legs: list[oc.Leg(kind, strike, qty, premium)]
oc.plot.greek(name, spot_range, strike, expiry, rate, vol, kind="both")
```

All scalar paths accept `kind="call"` or `kind="put"`. All numeric inputs
(`spot`, `strike`, `expiry`, `vol`, `price`) accept either Python floats
or NumPy arrays — the dispatch is automatic.

## Quickstart with no account, no network

```python
import opticore as oc

chain = oc.fetch_chain(provider="sample", symbol="SPY")  # bundled fixture
enriched = oc.enrich(chain, rate=0.045, div_yield=0.013)
oc.plot.smile(enriched)
```

The `sample` provider ships a 28 KiB synthetic SPY chain inside the
wheel. BSM-priced with a realistic smile/skew. Date-rebased on load so
TTEs stay sensible. Use it for examples, CI, and tutorials.

## Source layout (where to read code, not where to edit blindly)

```
python/opticore/             # Public Python API — start here for API questions
  __init__.py                  price, iv, greeks, greeks_table, Leg, GreeksResult
  __init__.pyi                 type stubs (PEP 561)
  chain.py                     fetch_chain, enrich, parity_check, implied_forward
  chain.pyi                    type stubs for chain.py
  plot.py                      smile, payoff, greek
  plot.pyi                     type stubs for plot.py
  data/sample.py               bundled SPY fixture provider
  data/ibkr.py                 IBKR adapter (ib_async, ephemeral connection)
  data/yfinance_adapter.py     Yahoo Finance adapter

src/                         # C++20 core — DO NOT EDIT WITHOUT READING ADR-0001..0005
  bindings.cpp                 nanobind bindings (zero-copy NumPy ↔ std::span)
  bsm.cpp                      Black-Scholes-Merton pricer
  jaeckel.cpp                  IV solver (Newton + bisection)
  greeks.cpp                   analytic Greeks, single-pass
include/opticore/
  math.hpp                     norm_cdf, norm_pdf, norm_inv

tests/python/                # 280 tests, ~94% coverage
tests/cpp/                   # 96 tests / 1653 assertions, multiple RNG seeds
docs/decisions/              # 5 ADRs locking the major design choices
```

## What an agent should NOT touch without explicit human approval

- `tests/cpp/reference_values.hpp` — hand-verified at full precision
- The numerical tolerance ladder in `AGENT.md` § Testing accuracy
- Anything in `src/` C++ core — read ADRs first
- `python/opticore/_core.pyi` — generated from C++ bindings
- The 9 public function signatures — they're API contract; breaking them needs an ADR

## Common gotchas

- nanobind ndarray inputs require dtype=float64, C-contiguous, AND writeable;
  `np.require(arr, dtype=np.float64, requirements=["C", "A", "W"])` is the canonical coercion.
- `ib_async` calls `asyncio.run()` internally — patched in `data/ibkr.py` for Jupyter.
- IV solver returns NaN (not exceptions) for arbitrage-violating prices —
  see ADR-0002.
- Pandas 3.0+ `Series.to_numpy()` returns read-only views by default;
  `np.require(..., "W")` handles this.

## Deeper context

- `AGENT.md` — full project-context dump (architecture, gotchas, "where we left off")
- `ROADMAP.md` — Phase 2+ scope (vol surfaces, Heston, MC, etc.)
- `CHANGELOG.md` — release notes
- `CONTRIBUTING.md` — build & test commands
- `RELEASING.md` — maintainer release flow (gitignored, local-only)
- `docs/decisions/` — ADR-0001 through ADR-0005

## Tests

```bash
pytest tests/python/                                          # default suite, ~1.5s
pytest tests/python/ --cov=opticore --cov-report=term         # coverage (gate: 85%, currently ~94%)
pytest tests/python/test_benchmarks.py -m benchmark           # performance benchmarks (opt-in)
mypy --strict tests/python/typing/check_strict_api.py         # type-stub regression check
./build_and_test.sh                                           # C++ tests
```

CI runs all of the above on Linux + macOS + Windows × Python 3.10–3.13.
